coredata.dc:title: NH2-carbon dot and hydrogel based stationary phase for mixed-mode liquid chromatography
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tong
Liang, Xiaojing
Chang, Lijing
Lu, Xiaobing
Li, Shuning
Wang, Shuai
Wang, Licheng
coredata.dc:description: For high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) laboratories, expanding the scope of application of a column to the extent that it can substitute for two or even three single-mode columns are crucial for reducing laboratory operational costs. The mixed-mode stationary phase, which exhibits properties suitable for various applications, can to a certain degree meet this demand. Because the separation mode of mixed-mode stationary phase (which possessed multiple interaction sites) can be modulated by adjusting mobile phase condition. Consequently, the analysis and separation of hydrophilic and hydrophobic samples can be accomplished within a same column. In this research, we developed a novel stationary phase by functionalizing silica with NH2-carbon dot and hydrogel, which can be utilized in mixed-mode liquid chromatography. The successful fabrication of this stationary phase was validated through the application of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), among other techniques. Additionally, the evaluation of chromatographic performance revealed that this stationary phase can be effectively utilized in both hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) and reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) modes. The column demonstrated superior separation performance, including an improved peak shape and enhanced separation selectivity, in comparison to commercial NH2-column and Diol-column under HILIC mode. This was particularly evident when analyzing a range of polar analytes, including saccharides, nucleosides/bases and alkaloids in HILIC mode. In short, a novel mixed-mode stationary phase was successfully developed by integrating NH2-CD with hydrogel, thereby broadening the potential applications of hydrogels in HPLC. In addition, this stationary phase presents significant potential for the analysis of hydrophilic, hydrophobic, and complex samples. This allows a single column to serve multiple purposes, thereby reducing the operational costs of laboratories.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH2-carbon dot
Hydrogel
Functionalized silica
Mixed‑mode liquid chromatography

Van Deemter analysis and column efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD preparation route.

Illustrates the curves depicting the effects of (a) the volume fraction of water content (b) temperature and (c) buffer concentration on the retention factors of analytes and (d) the zeta potential of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD under different buffer concentration. Mobile phase: (a) ACN and different content 100 mM NH4AcO (b) 85 % ACN and 15 % 150 mM NH4AcO (c). 75 % ACN and 25 % H2O with different ammonium acetate concentration. UV detection at 254 nm. Column temperature:(a) and (c) 25 ℃, (b) 25 ℃ to 45 ℃.

Chromatograms for the separation of eight nucleosides/bases over (a) Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and (b) Diol-column, NH2 column, bare silica and chromatograms for the separation of seven alkaloids over (c) Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD (d) Diol-column, NH2 column, bare silica. Analytes:(a) and (b)1. thymine 2. thymidine 3. uridine 4. adenosine 5. Cytosine 6. inosine 7. 6-chlorouracil 8. guanosine; (c) and (d) 1. piperine 2. caffeine 3. theobromine 4. theophylline 5. berberine hydrochloride 6. tetrandrine 7. fangchinoline; Mobile phase: (a) and (b) 90 % ACN and 10 % 100 mM NH4OAc, (c) and (d) 90 % ACN and 10 % 100 mM NH4OAc.

Illustrates the curves depicting the effects of (a) ACN content (b) temperature on the retention factors of analytes and chromatograms for the separation of five substituted benzene compounds over (c) Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and (d) C18. Analytes: 1. benzyl alcohol 2. phenol 3. benzaldehyde 4. benzoic acid 5. nitrobenzene Mobile phase: (b) 10% ACN and 90% H2O (c) 15% ACN and 85% H2O (d) 50% ACN and 50% H2O.

(a) LSER coefficients of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD, NH2 column, C18 column, Diol- column and (b) plots of column plate height versus flow rate curves for caffeine and thymine and (c) chromatograms of actual sample. Mobile phase: (a) 90 % ACN and 10 %150 mM NH4AcO, (b) 88 % ACN and 12 % 100 mM NH4OAc and (c) 26 % ACN and 74 % H2O.

head.ce:title: NH2-carbon dot and hydrogel based stationary phase for mixed-mode liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Petroleum Technology Research Institute, PetroChina Changqing Oilfield Company, Xi ’an 710018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Petroleum Technology Research Institute, PetroChina Changqing Oilfield Company, Xi ’an 710018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) laboratories, expanding the scope of application of a column to the extent that it can substitute for two or even three single-mode columns are crucial for reducing laboratory operational costs. The mixed-mode stationary phase, which exhibits properties suitable for various applications, can to a certain degree meet this demand. Because the separation mode of mixed-mode stationary phase (which possessed multiple interaction sites) can be modulated by adjusting mobile phase condition. Consequently, the analysis and separation of hydrophilic and hydrophobic samples can be accomplished within a same column. In this research, we developed a novel stationary phase by functionalizing silica with NH2-carbon dot and hydrogel, which can be utilized in mixed-mode liquid chromatography. The successful fabrication of this stationary phase was validated through the application of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), among other techniques. Additionally, the evaluation of chromatographic performance revealed that this stationary phase can be effectively utilized in both hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) and reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) modes. The column demonstrated superior separation performance, including an improved peak shape and enhanced separation selectivity, in comparison to commercial NH2-column and Diol-column under HILIC mode. This was particularly evident when analyzing a range of polar analytes, including saccharides, nucleosides/bases and alkaloids in HILIC mode. In short, a novel mixed-mode stationary phase was successfully developed by integrating NH2-CD with hydrogel, thereby broadening the potential applications of hydrogels in HPLC. In addition, this stationary phase presents significant potential for the analysis of hydrophilic, hydrophobic, and complex samples. This allows a single column to serve multiple purposes, thereby reducing the operational costs of laboratories.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) serves as an analytical instrument across a multitude of sectors, including environmental, chemicals, and biomedical analysis, etc. [1]. However, the separation and analysis of various substances often necessitates the numerous distinct chromatographic columns due to the single separation mechanism which an escalation in laboratory operational costs [2,3]. This issue may be mitigated by employing mixed-mode stationary phase. In contrast to single-mode chromatography, which provides a singular separation mechanism, the separation mode of mixed-mode stationary phase (which possessed multiple interaction sites) can be modulated by adjusting mobile phase condition [4,5]. Consequently, the analysis and separation of hydrophilic and hydrophobic samples can be accomplished within a same column. In recent years, a plethora of innovative mixed-mode stationary phases have been prepared and subsequently employed for the separation of intricate samples. Notably, functionalized silica stationary phases have been extensively researched, with ionic liquid [6,7], covalent organic frameworks (COF) [8–10], and metal organic frameworks (MOF) [11,12] serving as modifiers. However, the variety of mixed-mode stationary phases remains limited which possessed excellent separation performance. In addition, Chinese herbal medicine, a significant component of traditional Chinese medicine, boasts a rich history and profound cultural heritage. This invaluable resource has been bestowed upon humanity by nature. It plays a crucial role in disease prevention and treatment. There is an urgent demand for the creation of innovative mixed-mode stationary phases. These could potentially decrease laboratory operational costs and be applied to the analysis of Chinese herbal medicine.

Hydrogel, a hydrophilic polymer characterized by its three-dimensional network structure, has garnered significant interest across diverse fields across sensors [13], medicine [14] and 3D printing [15], because of its convenient shaping ability, functional designability, multiple interaction sites and biocompatibility. Furthermore, hydrogels are an optimal choice for modifying silica as a chromatographic stationary phase due to their abundant functional groups, including amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc. Preliminary studies have been conducted on this subject and the obtained stationary phase exhibited good separation performance [16–18]. Furthermore, the chromatographic separation performance of the stationary phase can be further enhanced by integrating hydrogel with other modifiers. For example, we synthesized MOF-assisted hydrogel modified silica stationary phase by incorporating MOF nanoparticles into the pre-polymerization solution of the hydrogel [19,20]. The obtained composited hydrogel functionalized silica stationary phase exhibited superior performance compared to hydrogel modified silica without the addition of MOFs.

Carbon dots (CDs) are surface-functionalized carbon nanoparticles typically ranging in diameter from 2 to 10 nm [21]. Recently, the exceptional stability and low toxicity of CDs have led to their increased use in the creation of chromatographic stationary phases [22,23]. In this study, chitosan (CS), which contains a significant number of hydroxyl and amino groups, and dopamine, known for its self-polymerization in both acidic and alkaline solutions and superior adhesion properties, were selected as the hydrogel monomers, while NH2-CD was employed as an additive to functionalize silica. The resulting stationary phase, due to its multiple interaction sites (amino, hydroxyl, benzene ring, NH2-CD), is suitable for use in mixed-mode chromatographic separation. Additionally, the investigation of chromatographic performance demonstrated that this stationary phase provided better separation performance than commercial reference columns.

Silica (UniSil 5–100, 5 µm, 10 nm) was procured from Suzhou Nanomicro Technology, China. The chemicals used in this study, including ammonium acetate, CS, and dopamine were sourced from Aladdin Co., Ltd. The NH2 packing (Gemsil NH2, 5 μm, 12 nm) and Diol packing (Diol 5 µm, 10 nm) was provided by Anavo Technologies Inc., China. In addition, they were slurry-packed into a stainless-steel column (150 × 4.6 mm) by the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The C18 column (JADE-PAK AF-C18, 5 µm, 12 nm) was purchased form Taiwei Technology Co. Ltd. Acetonitrile (ACN) was purchased from Por Mee. Glutaraldehyde (25 % in water) and ε-Poly-L-lysine (MW 3800–4200) were obtained from Innochem. All test samples utilized in this research were either analytical reagent grades or standard samples, and were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. More details can be found in the supporting information.

The NH2-CD was synthesized utilizing a simple pyrolysis method by choosing ε-Poly-L-lysine as the precursor [24]. Furthermore, the NH2-CD solution was procured following a temperature regulation procedure, centrifugation, and dispersion. More details can be found in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and column packing
ce:section.ce:para: The innovative stationary phase was synthesized using a straightforward method that incorporated physical coating as depicted in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the silica was activated as reported method [19]. Then, 0.15 g CS and 5 mL NH2-CD solution were added to a 100 mL round-bottom flask containing 45 mL deionized water. Upon the addition of 1 mL acetic acid, the mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring for a duration of 10 min to facilitate the dissolution of CS. Subsequently, 0.1 g dopamine, 1 mL glutaraldehyde (25 % in water), and 3 g activated silica were added to the above solution and stirred for 20 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture solution. Following this, the round-bottom flask was immersed in an oil bath and heated to 40 ℃ and maintained for 5 h. The temperature was then elevated to 115 ℃ to facilitate the evaporation of deionized water, which resulted in the formation of an NH2-CD doped hydrogel coating on the silica surface. The product was then submerged in deionized water for a duration of 12 h, followed by three washes using centrifugation with water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, the synthesis of NH2-CD and hydrogel modified silica was achieved by subjecting them to a drying process at 80 ℃ for 12 h in a vacuum oven. Furthermore, the hydrogel functionalized silica (Sil@CS/PDA) was synthesized using identical procedures, but without the inclusion of NH2-CD. The stationary phase was slurry-packed into a stainless-steel column (150 × 4.6 mm) [25].

The surface morphology of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD were examined using SEM and contrasted with those of bare silica. As depicted in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD surface exhibits greater roughness than that of the bare silica. Furthermore, a distinct hydrogel coating is evident on the silica surface following functionalization. The image of TEM further demonstrated that the silica was successfully modified by hydrogel (Fig. S2d). Moreover, the existence of NH2-CD on the surface of silica was confirmed using laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM). As shown in Fig. S3, the functionalized silica exhibited excellent dispersion and a blue fluorescent color under ultraviolet light exposure (400 nm). Additionally, the N2 adsorption–desorption experiment was conducted to examine the impact of modification on specific surface area and pore structure. As illustrated in Fig. S4a, both unmodified silica and Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD exhibited type-IV isotherms, suggesting a mesoporous structure. It is noteworthy that the BET surface area experiences a substantial reduction from 378.8 m2/g to 326.7 m2/g, and the average adsorption pore width decreased from 9.25 nm to 8.63 nm following modification. This indicates the formation of a hydrogel coating not only on the external surface but also within the pores of the silica. (Table S1). The thermal stability of the samples was further evaluated using thermogravimetric analysis. As depicted in Fig. S4b, the unmodified silica demonstrated a mass loss of 4.9 wt%, which can be ascribed to the condensation of silanol groups, releasing water. Moreover, Sil@CS/PDA and Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD, exhibited a notable mass loss of 12.1 wt% and 17.2 wt% respectively between 240 ℃ and 800 ℃. This can be attributed to the pyrolysis of the modifier on the surface of silica. The difference in mass loss between Sil@CS/PDA and Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD underscores the successful integration of NH2-CD. All the results of aforementioned characterizations unequivocally demonstrate the successful fabrication of a NH2-CD and hydrogel co-functionalized silica stationary phase.

The hydrogel coating, which forms on the silica surface, facilitates the creation of a water layer, which plays a crucial role in the separation process of HILIC mode [26,27]. Consequently, eight nucleosides/bases were chosen as model analytes to examine hydrophilic retention mechanism of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD by manipulating the volume fraction of water. It is evident that the retention factors of this analytes diminished as the water content increased (Fig. 2 a), demonstrating typical characteristics of HILIC. A model that integrates partitioning and adsorption can be selected to elucidate the influence of water content on retention factors [28].
(1) ln k = a1 + a2 ln (φ) + a3 φ

In the equation, k represents the retention factor, ai denotes the regression coefficient, and φ signifies water content in the mobile phase. It can be seen an excellent fitting result was achieved (R2 = 0.9901–0.9998) as the volume fraction of water increased from 15 % to 35 % (Fig. 2a and Table S2). In addition, the influence of temperature on rentention was examined by varing the temperature from 25 ℃ to 45 ℃. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the retention factors of analytes demonstrated a decline with increasing temperature. The dependence of ln k on temperature is given by Eq. (2) in the supporting information.The slope of the lines of ln k vs T−1 is −(ΔHθ)/R, so the decrease in the retention factors with increasing temperature indicates that the standard enthalpy changes (ΔHθ) are negative. Furthermore, the van't Hoff plots yielded highly satisfactory results (R2 = 0.9875–0.9994, Table S3), suggesting that the retention mechanism remained consistent throughout the temperature increase process.

Research indicates that the concentration of buffer salts can modulate the retention behavior of HILIC mode. This is achieved by adjusting the thickness and/or polarity of the water-rich layer, altering electrostatic interactions, and influencing hydrogen bonding [29]. Thus, in order to comprehensively examine the chromatographic behavior of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD, we investigated the impact of varying ammonium acetate concentrations (50 to 250 mM) on retention factors. As shown in Fig. 2c, with the increase of ammonium acetate concentration, the retention factors of the two nucleosides/bases show an increasing trend. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that as the concentration of buffer salt increases, so does the polarity and/or thickness of the rich water layer. Consequently, this leads to an extended retention time for hydrophilic analytes [30]. Nevertheless, as the concentration of ammonium acetate escalates, a discernible decline in the retention factors of the four organic acids is observed. This suggests the potential presence of ion exchange interactions. Consequently, the zeta potential of the stationary phase was investigated under different ammonium acetate concentrations. As shown in Fig. 2d, the stationary phase exhibited a positive zeta potential, suggesting the existence of an electrostatic attraction interaction between the organic acids and stationary phase. However, the value of zeta potential exhibited a decrease as the concentration of ammonium acetate increased. This reduction weakened the electrostatic attraction between organic acids and stationary phase, thereby diminishing the retention factor.

In order to further assess the chromatographic performance of the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD, the separations of various hydrophilic analytes were conducted and subsequently compared the results with those obtained from other hydrophilic stationary phase and bare silica. Nucleosides/bases have garnered significant attention in the realm of biological analysis and are currently employed to investigate biomarkers associated with tumor processes [31–33]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD effectively separated eight nucleosides/bases, demonstrating a better peak shape and higher resolution compared to Diol-column, NH2 column and bare silica. Moreover, the elution order of these nucleosides/bases did not correspond with their hydrophilicity (log Ko/w, Table S4). This suggests that multiple interactions, such as ion-exchange, hydrophilic, hydrophobic, and π-π interaction, may contribute to the separation process. Alkaloids, which are nitrogen-containing alkaline organic compounds derived from plant extracts, possess significant biological activity and serve as effective ingredients in traditional Chinese medicine. Consequently, the separation and analysis of alkaloids are crucial for the advancement of traditional Chinese medicine. As shown in Fig. 3c, the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD successfully separated seven alkaloids, exhibiting superior retention ability and separation selectivity compared to the Diol-column, NH2 column, and bare silica. Saccharides is a vital component in the human body, functioning not only as the primary energy source but also playing an instrumental role in maintaining essential life functions, forming bodily tissues, and promoting overall health. The separation of five saccharides was successfully accomplished using Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD within a 10 min timeframe, which was not replicated under identical mobile phase conditions with the other stationary phase (Fig.S5).

Furthermore, the separation efficacy of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and Sil@CS/PDA was juxtaposed to substantiate the beneficial impact of NH2-CD on the separation process. The variances in retention time and resolution of the three analytes on Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and Sil@CS/PDA suggest that NH2-CD was successfully integrated into the hydrogel and modified on the silica surface. A detailed comparison of the chromatogram reveals that cytosine and inosine cannot be baseline-separated using Sil@CS/PDA (Fig. S6a), similarly, berberine hydrochloride and tetrandrine were co-eluted (Fig. S6b). In the context of saccharide separation, Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD demonstrated superior performance. The above analysis implies that the incorporation of NH2-CD can provide additional interaction sites, thereby enhancing separation performance of stationary phase. Furthermore, Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD effectively separated five antibiotics (Fig. S7a) and five sulfonamides (Fig. S7b).

Four polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) were chosen as analytes to investigate the chromatographic behavior of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD in RPLC mode [16,34]. Fig. 4 a illustrates that the retention factors of these four polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons consistently decrease with an increase in ACN content, a characteristic behavior typical of RPLC. Furthermore, the correlation between the retention factors and the volume of ACN can be articulated using Eq. (2).
(2) ln k = c0 + c1 CB + c2 C2 B

In this equation, ci represents the regression coefficient, k signifies the factors of four PAHs, and CB denotes the volume fraction of ACN in the mobile phase. The retention of four PAHs on Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD was primarily dependent on the hydrophobic interaction between the PAHs and stationary phase, as evidenced by the excellent fitting results (R2 = 0.9921–0.9993, Table S5). Additionally, the influence of temperature on the retention mechanism of four anilines in RPLC mode was examined. The van't Hoff plots for retention factors and temperature yielded excellent fitting results (R2 = 0.9974–0.9990, Fig. 4b and Table S6), suggesting that despite temperature changes, the retention mechanism remains consistent.

The separation performance of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD was further investigated under RPLC mode. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD can effectively separate five substituted benzene compounds, the elution order of substituted benzene on Sil@CS/PDA/NH2 is same as C18 column. In addition, when Sil@CS/PDA/NH2 was used, less acetonitrile content was required to separate substituted benzene than when C18 column is used, more complex green chemistry concept. Furthermore, the elution sequence of five substituted benzene compounds was found to align with their log Ko/w values (Table S7), suggesting that hydrophobic interactions are pivotal in the separation process. PAHs and anilines serve as essential raw materials in the organic chemical industry, particularly for the production of dyes and plasticizers. Nonetheless, PAHs and anilines in the environment can adversely impact the quality of human life due to their genotoxicity, mutability and carcinogenicity. As illustrated in Fig. S8a and S9a, the separation of four PAHs and four anilines can be effectively achieved on Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD. More acetonitrile content is required to separate these compounds when C18 column was used, Fig. S8b and S9b. In addition, the separation performance of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and Sil@CS/PDA was evaluated under RPLC mode. The results indicate that the five substituted benzene compounds can be effectively separated at a baseline using Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD (Fig. S10a). However, Sil@CS/PDA is unable to separate these analytes due to the co-elution of benzaldehyde and nitrobenzene (Fig. S10a). Furthermore, the difference in elution order of analytes on Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and Sil@CS/PDA confirms that the addition of NH2-CD can influence the retention mechanism. Moreover, the enhanced separation of four anilines underscores the efficacy of NH2-CD in improving the separation performance of the stationary phase (Fig. S10b).

LSER is a dependable tool for assessing the retention selectivity of stationary phases and examining the interactions between stationary phases and analytes [35,36].
(3) log k = c + eE + sS + aA + bB + vV + d- D- + d+ D+

In the equation, k represents the retention factor. The lowercase letters are chromatographic system descriptors which represent π-π interaction, dipolar interaction, hydrogen bond donating ability, hydrogen bond accepting ability, hydrophobicity, and ionic interactions with both anionic and cationic solutes, respectively. The value of these descriptors can be determined through multiple linear regression. Coefficients that are positive or negative signify a stronger interaction between solutes and either the stationary phase or mobile phase, respectively. Given a fixed chromatographic condition, the performance of different columns can be evaluated and compared using coefficient values. The retention measurements were made under HILIC conditions, using the analytes listed in Table S8. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the negative value of v over Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD demonstrating a typical HILIC characterization. Furthermore, the Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD exhibited positive value of b, similar to the Diol-column and NH2-column, which are essential for HILIC chromatography. Conversely, regardless of the values for a and b, the C18 displayed a negative value, indicating the lack of sites for hydrogen bonding interactions. The Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD exhibited the highest e value, which is ascribed to π-π interactions. This can be rationalized by the structural differences between them; specifically, the presence of benzene ring in the PDA enhances its ability to interact with solutes through π-π interactions. Furthermore, the positive value of d- for Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD (the same as NH2 column) suggests the existence of an electrostatic attraction between the anion and stationary phase which is consistent with the results obtained from the study on the effect of buffer concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Van Deemter analysis and column efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the column efficiency of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD, the Van Deemter equation was used to describe the relationship between flow rate and theoretical plate height. As shown in Fig. 5b, caffeine and thymine had the lowest theoretical plate height of 18.81 μm and 23.16 μm at 0.3 mL/min, respectively.

Column efficiency serves as a crucial metric for assessing the performance of a column. Consequently, the column efficiency of representative analytes was determined in both HILIC and RPLC modes. As shown in Table S13, 3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid demonstrated the highest column efficiency of 35138 N/m in HILIC mode, and benzyl alcohol exhibited the highest column efficiency of 11885 N/m in RPLC mode under our operation condition. In addition, the column efficiency of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD was evaluated in comparison with existing research and demonstrated discernible benefits [37,38], Table S14.

White pepper, sourced from the dried and nearly ripe fruit of the pepper plant, is a type of Chinese herbal medicine renowned for its significant medicinal properties. It possesses various medicinal properties such as the ability to eliminate phlegm and detoxify the body, alleviate cold phlegm accumulation, provide anti-convulsant effects, dissipate heat, and exhibit anti-inflammatory properties. Consequently, the pepper extract was chosen as the actual sample. As depicted in Fig. 5c, two types of compounds were identified from the pepper extract under our operational conditions. Moreover, the retention time of peak 2 corresponds with that of the standard sample, suggesting that the primary component of the pepper extract is piperine. The HPLC-MS was used to further identify piperine in the chromatogram. As illustrated in Fig. S12 and Fig. S13, the peak observed at 5.089 min in the actual sample yielded a mass spectrum identical to that of the standard piperine observed at 5.048 min. This implies that Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD holds substantial potential for practical applications.

Quantitative ability serves as a critical important parameter to evaluate the performance of chromatographic columns. Consequently, a mixture sample (contained nucleosides/bases, alkaloids and organic acids) was selected as the analyte to investigated the quantitative capability of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD which is crucial for the practical application of HPLC. As illustrated in Fig. S11, both the peak area and peak height of the analyte demonstrated a proportional increase with the escalation of the inject volume. This finding indicated that Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD is suitable for both qualitative and quantitative analysis. Furthermore, the stability of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD were assessed using the identical analyte. As shown in Fig. S14a and Table S10, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the retention time for analytes fluctuated between 0.19 % and 1.20 %, following ten consecutive injections. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the retention time for analytes fluctuated between 1.32 % and 3.72 %, following 80 h continuous operation (Fig. S14b and Table S11). The above results indicated a good stability of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD. In addition, the reproducibility of preparation procedure was investigated by separating a mixture analyte over three different batches of Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD at lower concentrations i.e. 10 mM NH4AcO. As shown in Fig. S15, all three columns successfully separate the five analytes with minimal variation in retention time indicating the good reproducibility of the stationary phase in preparation and packing. In addition, the RSD of retention time of five analytes on three different batches was within 2.69–11.16 %, shown in Table S12.

In order to expand the scope of application of a column, we synthesized an NH2-CD assisted hydrogel functionalized silica stationary phase (Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD) which is compatible with both HILIC and RPLC mode. The investigation of retention mechanism indicated that multiple kind interaction sites were existed between stationary phase and analytes. In addition, Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD exhibited good separation performance for both hydrophilic and hydrophobic analytes, especially for the hydrophilic analytes such as nucleosides/bases, alkaloids and organic acids. Furthermore, a comparison of the separation performance between Sil@CS/PDA/NH2-CD and Sil@CS/PDA revealed that the incorporation of NH2-CD enhances the separation efficacy in both HILIC and RPLC modes. In addition, an excellent column efficiency of 35138.4 N/m was obtained for 3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid in HILIC mode. In short, a novel mixed-mode stationary phase was successfully developed by integrating NH2-CD with hydrogel, thereby broadening the potential applications of hydrogels in HPLC and could potentially reduce laboratory costs.

Tong Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaojing Liang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lijing Chang: Validation. Xiaobing Lu: Visualization. Shuning Li: Validation. Shuai Wang: Validation, Data curation. Licheng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Innovation Groups of Basic Research in Gansu Province (23JRRA570) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
